coredata.dc:title: Facile fabrication of nickel/porous g-C3N4 by using carbon dot as template for enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Puhui
Hong, Weisong
Cheng, Zhiwei
Zhang, Linping
Hou, Yu
coredata.dc:description: Ni/porous g-C3N4 was prepared by high temperature thermal polymerization process using carbon dots as soft template and photodeposition. With nickel nanoparticles supported as co-catalyst, the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) activity of the photocatalyst has been significantly enhanced under visible light, which is up to 1273.58 μmol g−1 h−1, superior to pristine g-C3N4 (4.12 μmol g−1 h−1). This is attributed to the inhibited recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and the much better electron transport efficiency. The formed porous structure of carbon nitride could facilitate light utilization and together with nickel nanoparticles, better charge separation can be realized which are proved by the photoluminescence, time-resolved photoluminescence spectra, transient photocurrent measurements and electrochemical impendence spectroscopy. This work provides a useful route to obtain less expensive and efficient photocatalyst containing no noble metals for hydrogen production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen evolution
Porous carbon nitride
Co-catalyst
Carbon dots
Photocatalytic

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of CN, PCN and Ni9PCN.
(a) and (b) TEM image, (c) HRTEM image and (d) elemental mapping image for Ni9PCN.
(a) XPS full spectrum and XPS spectra of Ni9PCN: (b) C 1s, (c) N1s, and (d) Ni 2p.

(a) The rate of H2 production over A, B and G and different loading amounts of nickel on CCN under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm), A: CN, B: PCN, C: Ni9CN, D: Ni3PCN, E: Ni5PCN, F: Ni7PCN, G: Ni9PCN and H: Ni11PCN (b) Photocatalytic hydrogen production over Ni9PCN.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum of CN, PCN and Ni9PCN and (b) Tauc plot of CN, PCN and Ni9PCN.
(a)The PL spectra, (b) Time-resolved PL spectra of CN, PCN and Ni9PCN.
(a) EIS Nyquist plots of CN, PCN and Ni9PCN. (b) Transient photocurrent response of CN and Ni9PCN.
Proposed photocatalytic hydrogen evolution mechanism for Ni9PCN under visible light irradiation.
Porous structure parameters of CN, PCN and Ni9PCN.

head.ce:title: Facile fabrication of nickel/porous g-C3N4 by using carbon dot as template for enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, Donghua University, 2999 North Renmin Rd., Shanghai, 201620, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, Donghua University, 2999 North Renmin Rd, Shanghai, 201620, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ni/porous g-C3N4 was prepared by high temperature thermal polymerization process using carbon dots as soft template and photodeposition. With nickel nanoparticles supported as co-catalyst, the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) activity of the photocatalyst has been significantly enhanced under visible light, which is up to 1273.58 μmol g−1 h−1, superior to pristine g-C3N4 (4.12 μmol g−1 h−1). This is attributed to the inhibited recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and the much better electron transport efficiency. The formed porous structure of carbon nitride could facilitate light utilization and together with nickel nanoparticles, better charge separation can be realized which are proved by the photoluminescence, time-resolved photoluminescence spectra, transient photocurrent measurements and electrochemical impendence spectroscopy. This work provides a useful route to obtain less expensive and efficient photocatalyst containing no noble metals for hydrogen production.

Non-toxic, easily removable carbon dots as a soft template to synthesize porous graphite carbon nitride. Non-noble nickel nanoparticles as cocatalyst. This nanocomposite exhibits superior photocatalyst hydrogen production performance.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Using semiconductor as a photocatalyst to drive water decomposition has attracted wide attention since 1972 when titanium dioxide was used for photoelectrochemical water splitting [1,2]. This makes it possible to utilize solar energy to solve energy problems [3–6].Several decades later, the carbonaceous materials obtained through different processes have become the research focus of scientists due to their unique properties [7–10]. Among them, graphite carbon nitride, which has excellent chemical stability and narrow band gap [11–13], once again set off a wave of photocatalytic decomposition of water. However, its undesired electron hole recombination rate and insufficient visible light absorption result in the low photocatalytic activity. To improve the optical and electronic properties of this matter, different strategies have been developed, including metallic and non-metallic elements doping [14] via high temperature thermal polymerization, heterojunction constructing by the combination with other compounds [15–17] and morphology and structure regulating [18–22]. In the preparation processes, different methods have also been employed, such as sonochemical method [23–27], templating method [28,29], self-assembly [30], precursor technology [31] and thermal decomposition synthesis [32], etc.

In photocatalytic systems, nickel compounds including nickel oxide [33], nickel sulfide [34,35], nickel phosphide [36,37] and nickel pyrophosphate [38] have been loaded on carbon nitride as photocatalysts in the decomposition of water, which displayed improved activity to produce hydrogen. Recently, Han [39] and coworkers reported a Ni/Mo alloy nanoparticles modified carbon nitride photocatalyst in which Ni/Mo component as a co-catalyst could replace the precious metal platinum for hydrogen production. It is worth noting that the nickel particles' valence is zero in this composite and it acts as an important part in the photocatalytic reaction, which illustrates that nickel metal can be a potential co-catalyst in the photocatalytic water decomposition process.

Porous g-C3N4 is of great interest in photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reactions since compared to the bulk g-C3N4, the specific surface area and the electron transfer efficiency are largely improved. Silica [40], SBA-15 [41,42] and MCM-41 [43] have been reported being used as hard templates to prepare mesoporous graphite phase carbon nitride. However, the hard-templating method requires the use of HF or NH4HF2 to remove the template, which is toxic and harmful. Soft-templating method is another option to prepare the porous graphite phase carbon nitride. Unfortunately, soft template agents, such as P123 [44] and sulfur powder [45], are not easy to be removed completely, which can affect the photocatalytic activity. In contrast, carbon dots, widely used in the field of photocatalysis [46], are non-toxic, cheap and easily decomposed at high temperature. Therefore, it can be a suitable choice as soft template to prepare porous g-C3N4 as in this work.

Herein, we report a Ni/porous g-C3N4 photocatalyst and its visible light-driven hydrogen generation activity. Ni/porous carbon nitride composite was prepared by loading nickel nanoparticles through photodeposition on the product of thermal polymerization of carbon dots and urea. Ni/porous g-C3N4 exhibited superior photocatalytic performance than Ni/g-C3N4. The effect of different amounts of nickel nanoparticles on the hydrogen production rate of photocatalysts were also studied. This work demonstrates that porous g-C3N4 with noble-metal-free co-catalyst exhibits great potential in light-driven water splitting.
All reagents were used as purchased. Citric acid monohydrate, NaH2PO2⋅H2O and Ni(NO3)2⋅6H2O were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. TEOA (triethanolamine) and urea was bought from Aladdin.

CDs were prepared by referring to the literature by a hydrothermal method [47]. Firstly, 1.0 g of urea and 3.0 g of citric acid were put into 10 mL of ultrapure water under stirring, then the solution was heated in a hydrothermal reactor at 180 °C for 5 h and a suspension was formed after the solution was cooled to room temperature, which was then centrifuged to get rid of the larger particles. Finally, the treated solution was freeze-dried and ground to powder for use.

Porous g-C3N4 was prepared by a one-step thermal polymerization method. 10 g of urea and 4.2 mg of CDs were uniformly mixed and put in a crucible with a cover, which was then placed in a muffle furnace and calcined at 550 °C for 3 h. The heating rate is 2 °C min−1. A product with dark yellow color was obtained and recorded as PCN. The preparation of carbon nitride (CN) was carried out under the same conditions as above except that no CDs were added.

Nickel nanoparticles were deposited on PCN by photodeposition. Trace amount of ultrapure water was mixed with 50 mg of the obtained PCN material, then the mixture was wet-milled and dispersed uniformly in 100 mL of 10% TEOA aqueous solution. Under vigorous stirring, A certain volume (3, 5, 7, 9 and 11 mL) of a solution containing sodium phosphite (0.1 M) and nickel nitrate (0.1 M) was gradually added to a quartz vessel, which was irradiated under UV–vis light for about an hour. The obtained suspension was filtered to get the powder, which was washed by using deionized water for several times and vacuum dried at 50 °C overnight. The obtained sample was recorded as NixPCN (x = 3, 5, 7, 9, 11 vol%). For comparison, Ni/g-C3N4 (NiCN) nanocomposite was synthesized by utilizing bulk g-C3N4 as a substrate.

The phase structures of all powder materials were characterized by powder X-ray diffraction by an 18 KW X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα irradiation. The surface morphology of the sample was observed by transmission electron microscope. The elemental composition of the sample was determined by an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. An ultraviolet–visible near-infrared spectrometer with an integrating sphere attachment was used to obtain the UV–vis diffuse reflection absorption spectra. The photoluminescence spectra were measured by a steady state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (QM/TM). The content of Ni in the sample was measured by a plasma spectrometer (Prodigy-ICP). The structural composition and chemical groups of each sample were determined by FT-IR spectroscopy. The detailed information of the instruments for each characterization can be found in the supporting information (Table S1).

Electrochemical and photoelectrochemical experiments were performed using a three-electrode system (Ag/AgCl: reference electrode; platinum wire: counter electrode; CN, PCN and Ni9PCN on the FTO: working electrode). 300 W xenon lamp was the light source. In the transient photocurrent response test, the applied potential is 0 V vs Ag/AgCl, and the electrolyte is an aqueous solution containing Na2SO4 (0.5 M) and triethanolamine in trace amount. For the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement, the voltage amplitude is 5 mV and the frequency varies from 0.01 to 105 Hz.

In the photocatalytic HER experiments, the amount of the photocatalyst used is 20 mg and the reaction media is 100 mL solution containing 10% triethanolamine. Photocatalytic online analysis system were employed to perform the HER experiment. Before starting the HER experiment, the vacuum system was generated. The light source used is a 300w xenon lamp with a UV filter (λ > 420 nm). During the reaction, a circulating cooling water system was applied to control the reaction temperature at 10 °C. The hydrogen amount was tested by a gas chromatograph equipped with a TCD.

Powder X-ray diffraction was applied to explore the phase structure of the photocatalyst. As seen in Fig. 1 a, the XRD patterns of CN, PCN, and Ni9PCN all have the diffraction peaks at 13.1° and 27.4° belonging to the crystal planes, (100) and (002), of carbon nitride [48]. When nickel nanoparticles were introduced, there was no shift at 27.4° diffraction peak, suggesting that the phase structure of carbon nitride has not changed, while the intensity of the diffraction peak became weaker, which is because nickel nanoparticles covered on part of the surface of carbon nitride. No significant peak of metallic nickel in Ni9PCN was found due to its low content in the material.

The FT-IR spectrum is applied to study the chemical functional groups of the sample (see Fig. 1b). There is a broad peak at 3000-3500 cm−1 which can be attributed to the –OH and N–H stretching vibrations [49], and the peak belonging to the stretching vibration of aromatic C–N can be seen at 1100-1800 cm−1 [50]. At 812 cm−1, there is a sharp absorption peak which is due to the bending mode of the tiazine-units [51]. However, after nickel nanoparticles were introduced, both PCN and Ni9PCN have absorption peaks similar to CN, also implying that the layered structure of CN has not altered.

The morphology and structure of Ni9PCN were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy. It can be seen from Fig. 2 a and Fig. S1 that the sample has a porous sheet shape. The porous structure is formed during the thermal polymerization process when urea is used as a precursor and carbon dots are employed as soft template agents. Carbon dots are decomposed at high temperatures to produce gas, which results in the formation of pores on PCN. The elemental analysis result of CN and PCN (Table S2) shows that the C to N atomic ratio of CN is 0.564, approximately equivalent to C to N atomic ratio of PCN 0.566, which implies that the main role of carbon dots is probably only to regulate the morphology of CN at high temperatures, but not to change the structure of CN. Fig. 2b is the high resolution TEM image of the material. The pores have diameters of about 20–25 nm. The distance of 0.203 nm between two adjacent stripes can be observed (Fig. 2c), corresponding to the (111) lattice plane of metallic nickel [52]. In addition, the EDX element mapping image of the Ni9PCN sample (Fig. 2d) further indicates that the nickel nanoparticles are successfully deposited and distributed in clumps on the porous carbon nitride surface. The EDS analysis results of CN, PCN and Ni9PCN are provided in Fig. S2, Fig. S3 and Fig. S4. In all samples, the peaks of C, N and O elements are present and the peak belonging to Ni element is also shown in Ni9PCN. The oxygen peaks in CN and PCN are attributed to the adsorbed water on the sample surface. For Ni9PCN, the oxygen peak is partly attributed to the adsorbed water on the surface, and the other part comes from the oxide of a small amount of nickel nanoparticles.

The nitrogen isotherm adsorption-desorption curves and pore structure parameters of the photocatalysts CN, PCN and Ni9PCN are shown in Fig. S5 and Table 1 , respectively. The types of adsorption-desorption isotherms of all photocatalysts belong to typical IV curves, indicating that the sample materials are mesoporous materials. The specific surface area of PCN is 56.003 m2 g−1, which is higher than that of CN (49.955 m2 g−1). The specific surface area of Ni9PCN is 47.603 m2 g−1, and its reduced specific surface area is attributed to the loading of nickel nanoparticles. In addition, the pore size of PCN (3.401 nm) is larger than that of CN (3.058 nm), due to the effect of carbon dots acting as a soft template.

The full spectrum of the X-ray photoelectron spectrum of Ni/porous g-C3N4 sample is given in Fig. 3 , in which elements C, N, Ni and O were all found. Fig. 3b is the C 1s spectrum, and the binding energies at 284.5 eV and 288.1 eV are due to the adventitious carbon on the carbon nitride surface and sp2 carbon in the s-triazine ring, respectively [53]. In Fig. 3c, four peaks at 395.3 eV, 396.5 eV, 397.6 eV and 400.9 eV are present in the N 1s spectrum, representing the nitrogen atom of C–N–H, CN–C and N-(C)3 groups and charging effects [54]. The peaks of the Ni 2p spectra at 821.1 eV and 869.3 eV can be assigned to Ni(0) 2p3/2 and Ni(0) 2p1/2 [52], and the binding energies at 878.0 eV and 858.1 eV belong to Ni2+ 2p1/2 and Ni2+ 2p3/2 [55], respectively. XPS analysis showed that Ni nanoparticles have been successfully deposited on the PCN surface.

The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance of samples is shown in Fig. 4 a. In the absence of a co-catalyst, CN and PCN exhibits very low photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity. When nickel nanoparticles are deposited on the PCN surface, the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity of Ni/porous g-C3N4 is significantly improved. The effect of loading amount of nickel nanoparticles on the carbon nitride surface is investigated. The deposition amount of nickel nanoparticles is adjusted by adding different volume of nickel ion solution during photodeposition. When the volume of nickel ions solution added was 9 mL (ICP analysis of Ni9PCN content, Table S3), the composite Ni9PCN had the best photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity of 1273.58 μmol g−1 h−1. In contrast, that of Ni9CN is only 462.42 μmol g−1 h−1. The higher performance of Ni9PCN is due to the fact that porous structure of carbon nitride could promote light absorption. The photocatalytic activity of PCN is 5.59 μmol g−1 h−1 in the absence of nickel nanoparticles as co-catalyst, suggesting that nickel nanoparticles are active centers. As the volume of nickel ion solution increased to 11 mL, the catalytic activity of the nanocomposite decreased. There are two possible reasons to explain this: one is the shielding effect of nickel nanoparticles since excessive nickel nanoparticles could affect the light absorption of the composite; the other is that overcharged nickel nanoparticles partially become electron-hole recombination centers, resulting in a decreased photocatalytic activity [46,56]. Fig. 4b is the photocatalytic stability test result, from which it can be seen that the photocatalyst still has good catalytic performance after two cycles. The decreased photocatalytic performance is probably due to that Ni nanoparticles are slightly leachated from the surface of PCN.

The optical properties of the materials are examined by using UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy in Fig. 5 a. The light absorption capabilities of PCN and Ni9PCN were compared with CN. The visible light absorption region of Ni9PCN is larger and the absorption intensity is stronger, suggesting that the light utilization efficiency is improved, so that more photogenerated electrons can be utilized to produce hydrogen. The band gap values of the materials calculated according to the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum were 2.65 eV, 2.28 eV, and 2.21 eV, respectively (Fig. 5b).

Photoluminescence spectroscopy is used to study the charge transfer and separation efficiency of photocatalysts. The stronger the fluorescence intensity is, the easier the electron-hole pair recombination is. From Fig. 6 a, it can be observed that the fluorescence intensity of the photocatalyst Ni9PCN is weakened when introducing the nickel nanoparticles, that can be explained by the suppression of electron-hole pair recombination in the photocatalyst. The porous structure in PCN shortens the carrier transmission distance and time from the bulk phase to the surface. The time-resolved photoluminescence spectra of samples CN, PCN and Ni9PCN are shown in Fig. 6b, and the corresponding attenuation curve fitting τav is calculated as 5.389 ns, 1.677 ns and 1.085 ns, respectively. The lifetime of PCN is shorter than that of CN, indicating that the porous structure promotes the electron transfer. Moreover, the lifetime of Ni9PCN is shorter than that of PCN, suggesting that faster electron transfer is achieved between nickel nanoparticles and CN, thus more electrons participate in the hydrogen evolution reaction.

The photogenerated charge separation and charge transfer performance are investigated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and transient photocurrent response. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the arc of Ni9PCN was the smallest, suggesting a faster electron transfer rate, and hindered recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pair [57–59]. In addition, Ni9PCN exhibited a higher photocurrent density (Fig. 7b) when compared to CN, further indicating that the photogenerated electron-hole separation efficiency is remarkably enhanced. Combined with the EIS analysis results and the transient photocurrent response, it can be concluded that both of the porous structure and nickel nanoparticles could promote the electron-hole pairs separation, further enhancing the performance of the photocatalyst.

The photocatalytic mechanism of the ternary Ni/porous g-C3N4 composite is proposed based on the above analysis and discussions (see Fig. 8 ). Under visible light illumination, the electrons of photocatalyst in valence band are excited to the conduction band, and at the same time holes are formed in the valence band. The photogenerated hole portion is quenched by triethanolamine to suppress recombination of electron holes. The porous structure facilitates the visible light utilization and accelerate the transfer of electrons. The electrons are moved to the surface of the nickel nanoparticles. The hydrogen ions acquire electrons from Ni to generate hydrogen.

In summary, a noble-metal-free Ni/porous g-C3N4 composite was synthesized in two steps via high temperature thermal polymerization and photodeposition. By adjusting the amount of nickel nanoparticles, the maximum hydrogen evolution activity of 1273.58 μmol g−1 h−1 can be obtained, which was approximately 319 times and 228 times of CN and PCN, respectively. Due to the positive effects of porous structure and nickel nanoparticles, this photocatalyst exhibited low electron-hole recombination rate, rapid electron transfer and efficient light utilization. It is worth mentioning that the composite material contains no precious metal, which offers a new strategy to design low-cost, high-performance, porous and stable photocatalysts.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China No. 21872025, China; State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials, Donghua University, KF1823, China.
